segments.s480-g49: برو ریخت is a past narrative action: here برو (literally “go”) combines with ریخت (past of ریختن, “pour/throw”) to form a single pictorial action meaning “he cast/strewn” or “he went and poured out.” دینار چندان is a noun + postpositive quantifier: دینار (coins) is specified by چندان (= “so many/much”), and ز گنج uses the poetic preposition ز (= از) to mark source (“from the treasury”).
segments.s480-g50: که introduces a result clause (“so that”), شد is the past of شدن “to become.” ماه را is topicalized/affected (را marks the definite noun as the party affected by what follows). راه رفتن is the gerund/infinitive phrase “walking the road,” and به رنج marks the manner/state “with hardship” or “difficult.” Put together: “so that (it) became for the moon that walking the road was hardship.” The poetic word order places the components for emphasis.
segments.s480-g9: بروبر is an adverbial/intentional modifier meaning 'directly/face-to-face' and here gives the manner of reading. بخواندند is past 3rd‑person plural 'they read/recited'; پس is an adverb 'then/afterward'. نامه را is the definite object ('the letter') marked by را. The sequence places the manner and verb before the object in a compact, poetic order.
segments.s480-g10: چو here functions as 'as/like' (a comparative/linking particle) introducing the clause that follows. پیغام آن شاه خودکامه را is a noun phrase 'the message of that self‑willed king' with آن (that) modifying شاه and را marking the whole phrase as an object/target of comparison; the clause explains the content or likeness to which the reading is compared.
segments.s480-g11: گزین کرد is a compound past ('chose'), where کرد functions as the light verb making the action past. پیران صد is the noun with numeral ('elders, a hundred') in a classical word order; از هندوان is the source phrase 'from the Indians' introduced by the preposition از.
segments.s480-g12: A string of adjectives joined by و gives qualities of the elders: خردمند 'wise' is primary, گویا here means 'eloquent' (not merely 'apparent'), and روشن‌روان is a compounded descriptive adjective ('bright‑souled' / clear‑minded). These adjectives follow the noun they modify in typical Persian descriptive style.
segments.s480-g13: در گنج بی‌رنج is a locative/prepositional phrase 'in the treasure without toil' (بی‑رنج = without toil/effort) that frames the action. بگشاد is a past verb 'opened' (classical b‑prefix past form) placed before its subject شاه in inverted poetic order; شاه is the subject 'the king.'
segments.s480-g14: گزین کرد again is 'he chose'; ازان 'from that/from it' refers back to the treasury just opened. A coordinated list follows as the direct objects: یاره (companions/attendants), تاج (crown), و گاه (and گاه — here functioning as 'seat' or 'throne' in the sense of a place/occasion of rule). The verb precedes the source phrase and the list of selected items.
segments.s480-g15: همان is the demonstrative 'that/the same' emphasizing identity. گوهر و جامه is a coordinated noun phrase 'gems and garments/robes'. نابرید is formed from نا‑ + برید (lit. 'not‑cut') and functions adjectivally here as 'unbroken/intact' (not an imperative); it qualifies the listed items.
segments.s480-g16: ز (poetic variant of از) introduces a comparative/source meaning 'than/from'. چیزی 'anything' plus the relative که introduces the subordinate clause. شایسته‌تر is the comparative 'more fitting/appropriate' and برگزید is past 'he chose.' Together the clause means 'than anything that he might have chosen as more fitting,' setting up the contrast with نابرید (he kept these intact instead of taking something else more suitable).
segments.s480-g17: ببردند is a past plural verb 'they carried/took' placed before its object (verb-first word order, common in poetic prose). سیصد شتروار بار is a quantity phrase: سیصد = 'three hundred', شتروار here functions as a measure 'per camel' / 'camel-load', and بار = 'load, cargo' — together: 'three hundred camel-loads' that are the object of ببردند.
segments.s480-g18: همان is a demonstrative/determiner 'that/the same' qualifying the following nouns. جامه و گوهر is a coordinated noun phrase 'garment(s) and jewel(s)'. شاهوار is an adjective after the noun (typical Persian adjective order) meaning 'regal/lofty', applied to the jewels and by context to the garments as well.
segments.s480-g19: The clause presents the subject first: صد اشتر 'a hundred camels'. همه بار دینار بود is the predicate with copula بود at the end: 'all (their) load was dinars.' Persian often places بود (was) at clause end to assert the state.
segments.s480-g20: Again صد اشتر opens the clause. ز introduces source/material: ز گنج 'from the treasure/treasury'. درم بار بود places the burden: 'they had loads of dirhams' with بود as the final copula. Word order emphasizes the carriers (camels) then specifies what they bore and its source.
segments.s480-g21: یکی introduces 'one' or 'a certain one.' مهد 'cradle' (here a container/heap) modified by the adjective پرمایه 'rich/abundant'. از + عود‌تر indicates material/source 'of/with agarwood (oud)'; the phrase as a whole describes one richly endowed chest/package of agarwood.
segments.s480-g22: برو here is a poetic contraction of بر او ('on it' / 'upon it') — an older idiom in this kind of verse — linking to the participle بافته 'woven'. بافته زر و چندی گهر therefore describes ornamentation: 'gold woven (on it) and some jewels.' The participle follows the (contracted) locative, and چندی indicates a small quantity.
segments.s480-g23: به introduces the indirect/locative idea 'to/on'; ده پیل 'ten elephants' is the direct object/thing placed; بر تخت زرین is a locative phrase 'upon the golden throne'; نهاد is the past tense of نهادن 'to place/put' (here 'placed'). The verb appears at the end, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s480-g24: This clause mirrors the previous structure: به پیلی گرانمایه‌تر 'on a more precious/valuable elephant' (پیلی used here as 'elephant'/'elephantine'), زین 'the saddle' is the thing placed, and نهاد is again the past 'placed.' Word order puts the location before the object and finishes with the verb.
segments.s480-g25: فغستان is a proper/place name personified here as the source of the rain. ببارید is the past tense of باریدن with the poetic prefix b- and means “fell/it rained”; its position after the subject follows standard classical Persian order (S V). خونین سرشک is a noun phrase where خونین (blood‑stained/blood‑red) qualifies سرشک (a teardrop); the adjective precedes the noun for poetic effect.
segments.s480-g26: همی رفت combines the uninterrupted/continuous past particle همی with the past verb رفت to give a sense of ongoing or habitual past action (“was going/kept going” or simply “went on”). با introduces companions (“with”), and the noun phrase فیلسوف و پزشک lists those companions using و as the coordinating conjunction.
segments.s480-g27: قدح is the goblet/cup and is the topic of this phrase. هم چنان here is an adverbial intensifier with چنان (“so/thus”) and هم adding emphasis; together they mean “thus/so (famed)”. نامداری modifies قدح as an appositive adjective/noun meaning the goblet’s renown. به دست literally “in the hand / held” uses به + دست to indicate possession or that the goblet was held.
segments.s480-g28: همه سرکشان is the plural subject meaning “all the (headstrong/proud ones)” and functions as the agent affected by the following predicate. از introduces the source/cause (“from / out of”), می‌جام is the wine‑cup, and مست is the predicative adjective “drunk/intoxicated”; the verb ‘to be’ is omitted (vernacular/poetic ellipsis) so the phrase reads “all the proud were drunk from the wine-cup.”
segments.s480-g29: چو آمد is a temporal clause meaning “when … came”: چو = “when” and آمد is past tense “came.” به marks motion toward or arrival at the person indicated (the king), and مشکوی is an adjectival form meaning “musk‑scented” modifying شاه (king). Word order is the classical S‑V plus prepositional phrase indicating destination.
segments.s480-g30: یکی works as an indefinite numeral/particle here (“a certain” / “one”), followed by تاج “crown.” بر سر literally “upon the head” uses the preposition بر + سر. ز (poetic for از) introduces the material or source (“of/from”), so ز مشک سیاه describes the crown as made of or scented with black musk; the adjective سیاه follows the noun مشک.
segments.s480-g31: بسان is a simile marker meaning “like/as.” گل زرد is a noun + adjective sequence (“a yellow flower”); note classical Persian permits adjective either before or after the noun, and here زرد follows گل. بر ارغوان is a prepositional phrase “upon/against crimson” giving the visual comparison background.
segments.s480-g32: ز here is causal (“from/because of”); دیدار او means “his/that meeting/seeing (of him).” شاد شد is a compound of the adjective شاد “glad/joyful” with the past auxiliary شد “became,” so “became joyful.” ناتوان is a final adjective meaning “incapable/overcome/weak,” describing the state produced by that joy; the sequence reads “became joyfully overcome/helpless.”
segments.s480-g1: This clause is subject + past verb: فرستاده (the messenger) + برگشت (returned). زان is an archaic compound of زِ + آن meaning 'from that', and مرز و بوم is a coordinated noun phrase 'border and land' (i.e. borderland). Word order is topic/subject then verb then source phrase.
segments.s480-g2: بیامد is past tense 'came'; به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'to/near' (به + نزدیک). پیران here is a proper name (Piran) and روم specifies his realm 'of Rum/Byzantium'. The clause continues the action: he came up to that person/place.
segments.s480-g3: چو at the start is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آن موبدان is the subject 'those mobeds (Zoroastrian priests)'. پاسخ شهریار is a genitive construction where پاسخ (answer/reply) is qualified by شهریار (the king): 'the king's reply'. This segment sets up a temporal subordinate clause.
segments.s480-g4: بدیدند is the past plural 'they saw'. با introduces accompaniment 'with' and attaches to موبدان (they were accompanied by). رنج‌دیده is a compound adjective 'rûnig-dide' = 'suffering / weather-beaten / weary', modifying سوار 'horseman / rider'. Together: 'they, with the weary rider, saw ...'.
segments.s480-g5: از ایوان is a source phrase 'from the iwan/portico'. به نزدیک شاه آمدند is the motion clause 'they came near the king' (به نزدیک = 'to the vicinity of', شاه = king). آمدند is past plural 'they came', so the actors from the previous clause are continuing action toward the king.
segments.s480-g6: بران is an archaic deictic 'upon that / to that place' and names the target court. نامور is an adjective 'renowned', modifying بارگاه 'court/palace'. آمدند repeats the past plural verb 'they came', emphasizing arrival at the famed court.
segments.s480-g7: سپهدار هندوستان is a noun phrase 'the sepahdar (commander) of Hindustan'. شاد شد is a two-word predicate where شاد is 'glad' and شد is the past of شدن 'became' — here 'became glad' = 'rejoiced'. The clause states the emotional reaction.
segments.s480-g8: که introduces a subordinate clause giving the reason ('that' / 'for'). از رنج اسکندر is a prepositional phrase 'from Alexander's hardship/oppression' (رنج + proper name اسکندر). آزاد شد is passive/agentless 'became free' / 'was freed' — together: 'that he was freed from Alexander's suffering', explaining why he rejoiced.
segments.s480-g33: 'چو' introduces a simile ('like/as'); 'سرو سهی' is a noun + poetic epithet (literally 'cypress dark') meaning 'dark cypress'; 'بر سرش' = 'upon her head' (preposition + noun + possessive clitic); 'گرد ماه' here reads as 'circle/halo of the moon' (گرد used in the sense of 'around/encircling' + 'ماه' moon). Word order is typical Persian: simile phrase followed by the locative phrase describing where that image appears.
segments.s480-g34: نشایست کردن is a verbal phrase: 'نشایست' (= proper/fitting) plus the light verb 'کردن' to form an impersonal/infinitival sense 'it was fitting/to be fitting to...'. 'به مه بر نگاه' is the prepositional complement meaning 'to/at the moon with a gaze' (به + مه, then بر نِگارشی 'بر' marks the object of the gaze and 'نگاه' = 'gaze'). The whole segment states the judgment that turning the gaze to the moon was appropriate under that condition.
segments.s480-g35: 'دو ابرو کمان' = 'two brows like bows' (ابرو 'brow' modified by کمان 'arc/bow'); the coordinating 'و' links the next phrase; 'دو نرگس دژم' = 'two narcissi (i.e. two beautiful/symbolic eyes) described as دژم 'sullen/grim'. Adjective follows the noun in the second phrase, as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s480-g36: 'سر زلف را' uses را as the definite/object marker: 'the head (or tip) of the tress' is the marked noun; 'تاب‌داده' is a past participial/verbal compound meaning 'has given a turn/has swung' (تاب + داده) functioning as the verb 'has bent/has twisted'; 'به خم' = 'into a curve'. Syntactically the phrase attributes the bending/curving action to the hair (poetic agency), producing the image of the head/pose thrown into a bend.
segments.s480-g37: This phrase is a poetic noun phrase: 'دو چشمش' = 'his two eyes' (numeric + possessed noun), followed by the simile marker 'چو' + noun phrase 'دو نرگس اندر بهشت' = 'like two narcissi in paradise.' Note the archaic locative preposition 'اندر' (= in/within) placed before 'بهشت.' Word order is typical Persian (possessor after numeral + noun), and the simile 'چو' links the eyes to the image of narcissi.
segments.s480-g38: 'تو گفتی' is the simple past second-person ('you said') introducing reported speech. 'که' is the complementizer 'that' introducing the content of what was said. In the embedded clause the preposition 'از' (here 'of/from') marks source/quality, so 'از ناز دارد سرشت' literally inverts the normal order (poetic inversion): 'سرشت دارد از ناز' — 'her nature has/of coquetry.' 'دارد' is the present tense 'has.' Note the poetic inversion where the subject/predicate order is shifted for metrical reasons.
segments.s480-g39: 'سکندر' is the subject (Alexander). 'نگه کرد' is a light verbal phrase where 'نگه' (gaze/look) combines with the past verb 'کرد' to mean 'he looked' or 'he fixed his gaze' — common poetic/periphrastic construction. 'بالای اوی' is a prepositional phrase using the archaic 'بالای' = 'upon/over' + 'اوی' = 'her' (possessive/pronominal), so 'he fixed his gaze upon her.'
segments.s480-g40: 'همان' here is an emphatic demonstrative ('that very' / 'the same'). A series of nouns 'موی و روی و سر و پای' (hair and face and head and foot) is listed and then closed by the pronoun 'اوی' = 'hers,' which attaches to the whole series; this is a typical Persian prosodic listing where the possessed parts come before the possessive pronoun. The conjunction 'و' simply links the items in the list.
segments.s480-g41: 'همی گفت' is a habitual/continuous past construction: همی marks repeated or ongoing action and گفت is the simple past 'said'. 'کاینت چراغ جهان' is a vocative/nominal phrase: کاینت (کاین + ت) = 'this/that one' used to address someone, followed by چراغ 'lamp/light' and جهان 'the world' — together 'O (you) lamp of the world.' Word order is Verb (همی گفت) then the quoted vocative.
segments.s480-g42: همی again marks repeated action. آفرین here is a noun/exclamation 'praise, acclamation' and خواند is the past 'he sang/recited/uttered' (here 'recited/praised'). اندر نهان is an adverbial phrase 'in/within secret' = 'secretly.' The clause therefore means 'and he would secretly recite/praise.'
segments.s480-g43: بدان is the fused prepositional phrase (به + آن) 'to that' (direction/indirect reference). دادگر is an epithet 'the Just (One / ruler)'. کو is the archaic/poetic relative 'who', and سپهر آفرید is a simple past clause 'created the heavens.' Overall: 'to that Just One who created the heavens.'
segments.s480-g44: بران is an adverbial/demonstrative 'on/thereupon/in that manner'; گونه here refers to a facial feature (cheek/part of the face) and بالا is an adjective 'high/lofty' modifying it; و links items, and چهر آفرید is 'he created a face/appearance.' The sequence reads as a unit: 'he created (him) with that high cheek/lofty brow and face.'
segments.s480-g45: 'بفرمود' is an honorific/polite past tense verb meaning 'he ordered/commanded.' 'تا' here introduces a purpose/result clause ('that/so that'), and is followed by the subjunctive 'بخرد' ('whoever may buy' — third person subjunctive of خریدن 'to buy/obtain' used in purpose/conditional constructions). 'بدند' is an archaic optative/pluperfect-form verb used here in the sense 'they should/give' (poetic/older variant of دهند/دادند); together the clause means 'he ordered that whoever might buy [her], they should give (her).' Note the poetic order and older verb form.
segments.s480-g46: 'بران' is an archaic prepositional/adverbial element meaning 'to/for that' or 'upon that.' 'لشکر روم' is a genitive/nominal phrase 'the army of Rome' (Rūm = Byzantium/the Romans). 'موبد' is the noun 'mōbed' (a Zoroastrian priest) used as the direct object, and the final 'بدند' is the same archaic verbal form as above (here rendered 'they gave' / 'they assigned'). Grammatically this is a short clause 'بران لشکر روم موبد بدند' = 'to that Roman army they gave a mobed.'
segments.s480-g47: نشستند is the past 3rd-person plural of نشستن (“they sat down”). The clause continues with و (and) and the object او را (him + object marker). به آیین is a prepositional/manner phrase “in/according to the custom/ceremony.” بخواست is a past form of خواستن (“asked/desired”); note the poetic/archaic truncation of the ending (expected بخواستند) so the verb reads as singular-form morphology while the subject is plural. Together the clause means “they sat and requested/asked for him according to the custom.”
segments.s480-g48: به رسم is another prepositional phrase meaning “in/according to the rite/custom”; in classical/poetic Persian the ezāfe (رسمِ مسیحا) is often omitted, so مسیحا is treated as a genitive “of the Messiah/Christ.” و links the next noun phrase. پیوند راست pairs noun + postpositive adjective (noun then adjective) and means “the true/right bond/covenant.” Both nouns are governed by the preceding به, so the full phrase reads “in the rite of the Messiah and the true covenant.”

sentences.s480-s25: He scattered so many dinars from the treasury that even it became hard for the moon to walk the road.
sentences.s480-s5: Then they immediately read the letter aloud, just as the message of that self-willed king.
sentences.s480-s6: He chose a hundred elders from the Indians, wise and eloquent and clear‑souled.
sentences.s480-s7: The king opened the toil‑free treasury and chose from it companions, the crown, and the seat (throne).
sentences.s480-s8: He kept those same jewels and robes intact, rather than choosing anything that would have been more fitting.
sentences.s480-s9: They carried off three hundred camel-loads of the same royal garments and jewels.
sentences.s480-s10: A hundred camels — all their loads were dinars; a hundred camels bore loads of dirhams from the treasury.
sentences.s480-s11: One (bundle) — a cradle rich with agarwood, on which gold was woven and a few jewels were set.
sentences.s480-s12: He placed ten elephants upon the golden throne; on a more precious elephant he set the saddle.
sentences.s480-s13: From Faghestān there fell bloody teardrops; he went along with a philosopher and a physician.
sentences.s480-s14: The goblet, so famed, was in hand; all the proud were intoxicated from the wine-cup.
sentences.s480-s15: When Faghestān came to the musk‑scented king, a certain crown of black musk was on his head.
sentences.s480-s16: Like a yellow flower upon crimson, he, overcome, became joyful at his sight.
sentences.s480-s1: The messenger returned from that borderland and came near Piran of Rum.
sentences.s480-s2: When those mobeds, accompanied by the weary horseman, saw the king's reply.
sentences.s480-s3: From the iwan they came up close to the king; they came into that renowned court.
sentences.s480-s4: The commander of Hindustan rejoiced, for he had been freed from Alexander's oppression.
sentences.s480-s17: When a dark cypress–like moon circled about her head, it was fitting to turn one's gaze toward the moon.
sentences.s480-s18: Two brows like bows and two sullen narcissus-eyes; the tress had thrown the head into a curve.
sentences.s480-s19: His two eyes, like two narcissi in paradise; you said that her nature is given to coquetry.
sentences.s480-s20: Alexander fixed his gaze upon her; that very hair and face and head and foot were hers.
sentences.s480-s21: He kept saying, "O light of the world," and he would privately utter praises.
sentences.s480-s22: To that Just One who created the heavens, he fashioned a lofty visage and countenance.
sentences.s480-s23: He commanded that whoever might buy (her) should be given (her); to that Roman army they gave a mobed.
sentences.s480-s24: They sat down and, in accordance with custom, sought to receive him by the rite of the Messiah and the true covenant.
